OC-SORT

An observation centric approach to multiple object tracking

Yeseo Jeon, Josh Sampson

Overview

Our subgroup’s goal was to implement a tracker that could be used for animal behavioral studies. To do this, we decided to implement an extension of the SORT algorithm (see SORT), called Observation Centric (OC) SORT. We believe that OC-SORT is an ideal for an animal behavioral tracker, which must be able to handle heavy occlusion and avoid identity switching.

As its name suggests, OC-SORT aims to handle some of SORTs limitations by focusing on new observations, rather than heavily relying on the model of motion. One limitation of SORT is that it requires a high frame rate to approximate linear motion, but this need for high frame rate leads to greater noise sensitivity in the motion model. Another limitation is that the noise in the velocity model will accumulate in the position model, which can especially be an issue when the object is occluded.

Observation-Centric Momentum (OCM)

Occlusions that can cause the accumulation of errors in regular SORT can be resolved with a step called observation-centric-reupdate (ORU) in OC-SORT. Once the object that was once occluded is observed again after a period of being untracked, the ORU step goes back and re-updates the filter’s internal beliefs about the object’s motion, by replaying the missed updates using a set of synthetic measurements called a “virtual trajectory”, instead of continuing to use parameters that were corrupted during occlusion.

Observation-Centric Re-update (ORU)

Occlusions that can cause the accumulation of errors in regular SORT can be resolved with a step called observation-centric-reupdate (ORU) in OC-SORT. Once the object that was once occluded is observed again after a period of being untracked, the ORU step goes back and re-updates the filter’s internal beliefs about the object’s motion, by replaying the missed updates using a set of synthetic measurements called a “virtual trajectory”, instead of continuing to use parameters that were corrupted during occlusion.

This virtual trajectory is generated using real observations from the detector right before the object was occluded, and in the first frame the object is detected again. The tracker then runs the KF update function along this trajectory, effectively correcting the filter’s internal motion parameters. This way, the ORU step minimizes the accumulation of errors that would otherwise continue to drift the algorithm’s predictions further and further away from the truth. Since the KF depends on previous predictions to make future predictions, without this step, the motion model would be inaccurate after occlusion, and the predictions will be off.

Project Code

You can view the full implementation of our OC-SORT tracker on GitHub.

View the project repository on GitHub

References

J. Cao, J. Pang, X. Weng, R. Khirodkar, and K. Kitani, “Observation-centric sort: Rethinking sort for robust multi-object tracking,” 2023 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR), pp. 9686–9696, Jun. 2023. doi:10.1109/cvpr52729.2023.00934
